title: 2026/03/05 - ENV (Part 1 of 2)
description: Thursday, 5th March 2026 | Environment Committee Planning Bill; Natural Environment Bill Hearing of evidence: Engineering New Zealand Dr Richard Templer, Chief Executive Annette Sweeney, Managing Director & Principal Environmental Engineer of Good Earth Matters New Zealand Initiative Nick Clark, Senior Fellow, Economics and Advocacy Dr Eric Crampton, Chief Economist Dr Bryce Wilkinson, Senior Fellow Dr Benno Blaschke, Research Fellow
descriptionSummary: Planning Bill. Planning Bill; Natural Environment Bill. Engineering New Zealand

chunks.text: So we are going to start hearings this morning on the planning bill and Natural Environment. Bill, welcome to Engineering New Zealand, uh, to you are our first submitter for the day. So, uh, welcome to our select committee. You've got 20 minutes with us this morning. So, uh, we'll hand it over to you for your opening remarks and introductions and then open it up for members questions.

So over to you. Thank you. Wonderful. Thank you very much, Kiara, everyone. I'm Richard Tempa. I'm the Chief executive, uh, of, uh, TGA Engineering New Zealand. And, uh, prior to that I was the chief executive at Manou District Council. Uh, on my left I have Annette Sweeney, who's the managing director and principal environmental engineer of Good Earth Matters.

Uh, Annette is a chartered professional engineer in three waters infrastructure and has extensive, uh, experience in strategic planning and consenting of said infrastructure. And on my right is Tanya Williams, our Chief Chief Engineer and General Manager member services.

And, uh, prior to joining us, she was the water and Waste General Manager at MWH Stand Head. So thank you very much for the chance. Uh, present engineering New Zealand represents the engineering profession in New Zealand. We represent professional engineers, technicians, and technologists, and have approximately 20 or 3000 members.

Engineers play a vital role in, uh, the consenting system In all its different forms. They are planners, regulators, designers, constructors, and shakers of infrastructures, infrastructure, building and construction. Um, we have general support for the bill. We strongly support the idea of strong integrated spatial planning 'cause that has the potential to revolutionize the way we plan, develop, and live in our communities, and it will deliver on the outcome sought by reform if it is done effectively and sensibly.

In terms of things we would like to see improved, first thing is we think the primary legislation needs to be be strengthened. Uh, too much has been deferred to the secondary legislation and later processes. We need to know what the national policy direction will look like and what is included as this is the foundation of the proposed system.

Uh, the na n sorry, natural environment build must include a goal for infrastructure critical to the national, uh, policy direction. An example of this, if you're looking at say, uh, the to live in highway that needs, uh, water rights in order for the infrastructure to be built.

Because you're dealing with huge amounts of stormwater, the legislation needs more detail. And in particular definitions, we need clear definitions of everything that is used in the bill. Otherwise it risks, uh, the courts trying to decide what is in and what is out. So now asked to Annette to talk about the timeframes.

Thank you, Richard. And thank you Committee. Um, engineering New Zealand understands that there is a real need to move to the new resource management system as quickly and efficiently as possible. Um, and as Richard has said, we strongly support spatial planning as a key foundation in the plan making process. We are very concerned about the proposed timeframes as well as the sequencing of the regional spatial plan in relation to setting of environmental limits.

And I know that you've heard several submissions on these matters, so we just wanna focus on a few points in terms of, um, implications for infrastructure. The timeframes for the RSP in itself are unrealistic, and there's a risk that that will not achieve the evidence-based strategic RSP that is visd, nor provide the certainty for enabling growth.

Infrastructure providers will be expected to input into the RSP in a meaningful manner to inform some of that mandatory com content. Um, one of those in particular being that it needs to include the existing and future key infrastructure, including corridors and strategic sites and opportunities to make better use of existing infrastructure.

Now for water service providers, this will need to be done at the same time that water services CCOs are being established and stood up and staff are transitioning into those new entities. Water services strategies will be completed probably midway through their RSP process, but the RSP will be developed ahead of things such like the stormwater network risk management plans and assessments to confirm the adequacy of stormwater and wastewater services.

So we think that there's a high risk of misalignment between the reforms under the RMA and the local water done well reforms. And that means that some spatial decisions may be locked in without reliable evidence on infrastructure capacity, affordability, and risk. There's a few further risk of community consultation overload and confusion with so many similar processes occurring concurrently.

We also think that there's a real risk of development exceeding environmental limits if the RSP proceeds ahead of setting environmental limits. Again, we are aware that you've heard several submissions on this matter. An example is in Hawke's Bay, where the future development strategy has identified water supply as a growth constraint.

And we also know that the aquifer is over allocated while we have an interim allocation through a proposed plan change. The actual environmental limit in terms of allocation of water is our known at this stage. We consider that things like the environmental limits need to be confirmed prior to the RSP being developed. And without this re-sequencing, we will not be able to be confident.

The RSP is consistent with environmental limits or sets the spatial implications of environmental limits as it's required to do so. So in summary, we strongly encourage the committee to make changes to the bills, to resequence the RSP to occur after environmental limits that will also enable more time for infrastructure providers to undertake the necessary work, particularly that which is directed under the local Government Water Services Act, so that they can inform the evidential and strategic basis of the RSP Re-sequencing will minimize the risk

of the RSP exceeding environmental limits and will also optimize the value of investment in water services assessment and planning that's required to be done under the local water done reform local water. Done well for I Pastor Tanya to talk on our next topic. Good Morning committee. I wanted to, uh, talk about livable communities From an engineering perspective, it's really important that development happens where infrastructure is there to support it and that hazards and climate risks are properly considered before this,

and that the urban environment remains healthy for those who live there. We importance of design and planning for communities so that they have a place where they want to live and have access to all the services. From an engineering perspective, the current drafts risks enabling development that will shaped primarily by market forces rather than requiring development, uh, to contribute to the positive lead to the resilience, health and social connections of communities.

Uh, we really need more than market forces. I have seen multiple examples in the years. One, uh, specific example was in Sydney, Australia, uh, where an apartment block was developed, where the stormwater system was unable to have it tapped in so nobody could actually live in the apartment block because the services and infrastructure was not there to support it.

These things are risks. We've seen development on, um, and New Zealand on flood prone land being allowed to, uh, for various market forces and on highly lfi uh, land still today. So we've, uh, outlined our specific recommendations and our proposal, but these are some from an engineering perspective. Hand back to Richard.

Thank you. Uh, the last point we'd like to cover off is the, uh, challenges of regulatory relief as proposed in the bill. Uh, we are really concerned that this will have a chilling effect, uh, in particular on local authorities not protecting areas of significant value for heritage cultural environmental reasons, because they'll be concerned of potential financial implications.

We understand the intent, particularly in situations where landowners face significant losses of to highly productive land or are unable to use large proportions of their property. However, you could end up in a situation where local government is meeting the sandwich between two acts on one hand, regulatory relief on the other hand, a requirement by the crown to take action.

The example I would use of that is the has no act, which requires local government to identify hazardous sites. Uh, an example I can think of in my past career was the identification of a, um, property development which was occurring on a site which had previously been a petrol station.

Now, the, uh, because of that particular activity under the has no act, that site is designated as a has no site goes into the, has no register, and that is permanently recorded on the property file for that piece of property, which substantially reduces the value of that property because that's something people are going to react to if regulatory relief applied in this case, um, you create this disincentive for council to carry out the intent of the has no act because it might be facing potentially hundreds of thousands

of dollars, uh, in regulatory relief. So we think, uh, that something more akin to section 85 of the existing RMA would probably be a better choice in this space. Thank you very much for your time. We're happy to take any questions. Thank You Richard, And thank you for those insights.

Um, look, we're gonna start with Rachel Brooking. Yeah, thank you. Thanks. This is a really helpful submission. Thanks for all the, um, consideration that's obviously gone into it. Uh, I'm interested in the infrastructure exception and, and you're saying that there needs to be more criteria around what can be, um, considered significant and then exempted.

Um, and then I see also when you're talking about, uh, natural hazards, uh, you are concerned about, um, clause 1, 4 6, um, where they don't, those provisions don't apply, that natural hazard provisions don't apply to upgrade maintenance of infrastructure.

But what I was wondering if you can do, sorry, this is my question finally getting there. Um, is you can, can you give us some examples of the sorts of things that you think, um, if it, if the criteria that you suggest were to exist would be significant infrastructure and be able to get through and, and maybe something that wouldn't?

Uh, so I'll start that and either of you feel free to jump in. Uh, key examples of infrastructure is where you have critical service infrastructure that is, is preexisting. So for example, if you think about, uh, water treatment and wastewater treatment for, for urban areas, that is an example of infrastructure that you need to have in some form.

So one of the challenges with the current, uh, RMA is if you are recon consenting a wastewater treatment plant or water treatment plant or something like that, often the starting point is what if this didn't exist at all? What if there wasn't, you know, 70,000 people living here that, that need these services?

So that to me is an example of where you have to take into account. There is a, a clear requirement for it for infrastructure, um, other forms of infrastructure where you might want to be looking. This would be, uh, less critical infrastructure. So possibly, uh, infrastructure which is being used not for, uh, you know, life safety reasons or something like that.

So it might be, uh, an additional roading network or, um, subdivision water feature or something like that. Thank you. Um, do you have, did either of you want to add to That? Um, I think just in terms of infrastructure, I guess one of the challenges with the bill is it's not defined anywhere in the bill other than in relation to designations.

Yeah. And that doesn't cover anything that goes into the water space. Um, and I guess also in terms of defining significant infrastructure, what I would say is one, one of the challenges that we have had in past practice is often it's defined around nationally significant, um, whereas I think regionally significant is actually quite important in terms of local water supplies, particularly in relation to, you know, like those lifeline um, services And, and that, um, natural hazards exception.
Can you explain the concern with that exception? Um, so that is on page 13 at the top of page 13.

Ah, that, that is essentially that natural hazards, um, doesn't apply to the construction upgrade maintenance operation of infrastructural or primary production activities. So our concern there is if you have natural hazard hazards, say flooding, earthquake, uh, or subsident risks, you need to take those into account when you are, for example, let's say building a large effluent pond on a, uh, a dairy site, building a wastewater treatment plant, putting in a cell cell phone tower or anything, you need to understand your natural hazards

and you need to make sure they are taken into account in both the design construction, but also the location. So the classic example is if you're looking at an effluent holding pond and you're in an area which I know well say the man were two, which is prone to flooding, you wanna make sure you put that outside of the likely overland flow path that you're gonna get in high rainfall Lot, you know, and, and also, um, is for upgrades.

So it's not just new infrastructure, but when, as we are, uh, needing to do at the moment maintaining and upgrading our current infrastructure, these are really important factors. Mm. And Cameron Luxon had a clarify, Well you answered it to Rachel Brooking about, um, definitions and you said that your primary or submission about, um, definitions of infrastructure being unclear, like how sort sort of definition in this bill, how do you think we could go about as a committee, um, for finding a way to get some definitions?

Well, I, I guess the first thing would be to include the infrastructure definition and the, the main, um, part of the interpretation, the act rather than just in relation to designations. Um, I think the RMA definition of infrastructure works well. Um, one of the challenges, I guess an example about where it doesn't work well is what's come out recently in the mp, the recent, um, national policy statements this year, um, with the addition of the term additional infrastructure, um, and how that relates to stormwater.

So that's created a lot of uncertainty. So, um, in terms of getting clarity for me, it would be actually having a definition of structure, which is overarching across the two bills. Thank you. And LA Thank you for your submission today. Um, I was interested in your suggestion about central government being liable for penalties.

And you know, I mean, as you point out, local government is, and you give the example of the defense force with, uh, PFAS. What, what, what made you raise this? Is this common internationally? Can you give us some more context about how it works? Uh, in, in this case, this one was raised particularly through, uh, personal experience of dealing with, uh, the defense force, contamination of the O Haki air base from, uh, P-F-A-S-P-F-A-S, uh, uh, basically, uh, nearly indestructible chemicals used in aviation fire foam.

There was a huge amount of contamination because firefighters had practiced, uh, simulating, putting out aircraft fires. Now what happened in that case is that, uh, the council working with really talented engineers found a solution for the local community, which was to put in a water source well away from the contamination and then pipe it to all the farms and houses in the area.

And that cost around $12 million to implement. The challenge was, is if that had been an airport, so let's say it had been a commercial airport, they would've had to fund that solution. If it had been any private business or in fact local government itself, they would've had to fund the solution because of the way the RMA ACT was worded.

There was, uh, the defense force could be prosecuted by horizons, but they couldn't be, there was no enforcement mechanism. They couldn't be required to pay, they couldn't be fined. Mm-hmm. So when the crown does something wrong, it would be extremely helpful if there was a mechanism requiring them to fix it.

Mm-hmm. And this seems now we were very lucky and the community owes a huge debt of thanks to David Parker, who was the minister at the time, who recognized that this wasn't the right thing. But the only reason that was, uh, mostly funded by, by the crown, partly by by local government, was because he recognized this was clearly unjust.

And, um, forgive the colloquialism beat up on treasury and the defense force to get the money out of them to pay for it. Mm-hmm. Pre anchor. Just a couple of very quick questions, but firstly, thank you. Very thorough submission and I found it really valuable. Um, you've mentioned, uh, that you'd support a system that has mechanisms to ensure that the environment isn't left worse off, uh, but ideally improved.

Um, but as far as I can tell, you haven't gone quite as far as to say that the goal states environment bill, uh, which is no net loss of indigenous biodiversity is not ambitious enough. Is, would you, would you support that being changed? Or where, where do you sit on that? I, I think, um, we've looked at this pragmatically and said that the, the goal is a, a pragmatic approach right at the moment, you know, I think all engineers would love to create situ uh, solutions where we could improve biodiversity, but in order to get to there, the first step has

to be stopping degradation. And so let's focus on doing that because there's a lot of situations where that isn't happening at the moment. But ideally, if we could have the building, the, the, you know, the, in the, the built environment improved by diversity, that would be, I think a good goal. I, I would say that is aspirational.

I think it would be very challenging to legislate for it because it would potentially substantially increase the cost of infrastructure. And I dunno that that's affordable. A quick Supplementary on that, just, just picking up on, and it goes back to the timing as well with you saying like, you know, the national direction needs to be clear before the regional plans.

Um, do you think there is a case where, uh, 'cause you point out that environmental outcomes could get worse if the bill just proceeds as is, um, do you think there could be scope for something like an overarching maintain or improve goal that sort of put that line in the sand in, in light of potentially national direction not being all fully in place?

Um, yeah, I think that would be helpful, um, as a backstop in case that national direction's not there. Yeah. Mm-hmm. Okay. Thank you. Thank you very much. Look, we have met time. We appreciate your time this morning and, um, those useful considerations and insights. So thank you very much. Very Helpful. Thank you. Thanks. So now moving on to our next submission this morning, uh, the New Zealand initiative who are here with us in person this morning.

Good morning, Bryce. Eric, uh, welcome to the select committee this morning. Um, you have 20 minutes with us, so we'll hand it over to you to, uh, talk us through your submission and then we'll open up the questions. Sure. Thank you and good morning everyone. Um, and thank you for the opportunity to present the submission, um, on these two bills, and particularly for allowing us to have 20 minutes, which is very unusual and, but it's great.

Um, so I'm Nick Clark, I'm a senior fellow at the initiative. Um, and with me this morning, uh, co-authors Dr. Bryce Wilkinson and Dr. Ben Leski, and also our chief economist, Dr. Eric Crampton, who also contributed to the submission. So I'll speak briefly to introduce a submission and we'll hand over, uh, to my colleagues so they can hit on some specific points of importance to us.

So look, we were very pleased that the government's intent has been to replace the RMA with the system based on property rights, on the enjoyment of property rights. And, um, property rights have been proven internationally to be crucial for economic growth and improved standards of living. Nobel Price for economics in 2024 reinforce that.

And, um, also, countries that don't respect property rights or the rule of law tend not to have good environmental records either, which is important. The RMA is an important, although not the only factor behind New Zealand's poor economic performance over recent decades for more than three decades, the RMA has un newly restricted land use, inflated housing costs delayed and made infrastructure horrendously expensive, and its compliance costs have impeded productivity, which has been dismal.

So we were very encouraged by the, um, national Act Coalition agreement commitment on RMA reform and the cabinets decisions in 2024, including its 10 principles for replacement legislation. The time taken to draft and introduce legislation was very quick. Um, but perhaps it was too quick, um, too constrained by a three year parliamentary term because unfortunately, despite the good intentions and public statements by the government, the bills themselves haven't hit the mark.

Why? Well, look, neither Bill mentions property rights as a purpose or among its goals. Regulatory relief provisions are in contrast, what some think not strong enough. Rather too strong the bills dropped, the need for a neat public benefit justification for restrictions by emitting entirely.

Section 32 of the RMA the bills confer far too much power on ministers who set national directions. And this matters because the goals of the legislation, the top of the funnel, so to speak, from which everything else flows, are multiple competing unre, unranked, and unconstrained by cost considerations.

Many terms from the goals are also vaguely worded at undefined in the bills. There is no architecture to operationalize the planning bills goal for competitive urban landmarks, which is a novel, uh, feature. We believe that litigation over the meaning of the goals and how they interact with each other is highly likely.

And this committee, however, can fix the bills by recommending some principled amendments to align them all with cabinet's. Good intentions and Bryce Ben Europe will talk through these. Um, but firstly I'll just hand over to Bryce. He'll talk about property rights and economic matters. Thank you Nick. Uh, chair and committee members.

Um, as Nick said, uh, we're concerned particularly about insufficient property, right? Protections and value for money disciplines in the bill goals should not be pursued regardless of cost. And the bills need to, to say that more generally. There are two fundamental principles that should guide government intervention that we think should be explicitly in the rules.

First, government should only intervene when doing so. Provides net benefits for the public, and that's the cost benefit analysis test. Secondly, intervention measures should respect property rights. And the Public Works Act shows, ah, that can be and should be done. These bills currently fail to embed either discipline.

First, they entirely remove RMAs section 32, national interest test. And second, they regulatory relief framework is inadequate. Um, to illustrate what is missing, um, consider the purpose statements in both bills. Both bills start out to say they establish a framework for pursuing A or B, depending on what the bill is.

We, uh, to focus your minds or illustrate our point. You could, each purpose statement could just add this qualifying clause at the end of both of them, uh, through measures that respect property rights and demonstrably provide positive net benefits for New Zealanders. So value for money test and a respect for property rights test.

And if you added those to the purpose statement, then officials would've to follow that through with measures in the bill. Well, this change would do is it would require transparent cost benefit analysis before setting national directions. And it would require analyzing the specific problems which we are seeing out in the community, which do justify the intervention and do explain what the intervention's meant to achieve what it's correcting.

And it would also provide a statutory anchor for challenging unjustified restrictions in a nutshell that would usefully change incentives. Uh, uh, one suggestion on the bill failing everything else is our put option as, as a, a fullback safeguard.

So where a regulation reduces land values materially through taking property rights or use rights owners could require their land to be purchased, um, by the authority doing this at an unimpaired value. And that would impose a real discipline on people making those decisions.

Those change, the changes we are suggesting here are about improving incentives to focus on net public benefits and win-win outcomes for living standards housing and the environment as nick property, right? So that important for solving treasury of the common and anti comments, problems, and for productivity growth.

Thank you very much. Now, now over to Ben. Um, we already mentioned that the bill is pretty much silent on explaining its own terms. It's a particular problem because, uh, we could, we could consider the rationale that we take, um, the terms in the bill from previous cases, that case law from the previous bill, the problem, the, the previous act.

But the problem is that courts will be interpreting the terms within the architecture that they're given. That is the act that they have been provided. If the act is silent, that would have to go about either silent or provide some kind of context that you might not intend. And if that's the case, then the courts will be either going through a long lengthy process of redefining and figuring out what is meant or it will be making conclusions that are wrong or not intended.

And in this particular case, I want to talk about competitive land markets, which is now considered a goal or supposed to be a goal in the act. There is no definition or explanation of what that means. So this particular problem, because that term is new, the concept is new, and it's intended to fundamentally change the economic logic of our resource management system.

Um, what is needed is not just, um, a definition, so, uh, basically in a interpretive clause, but like it's what was also required is an, an interpretive provision of the definition so that it's very clear that gives it operational content. The classic example is N-P-S-U-D. Um, so what we don't want is we don't want a definition going into national direction.

It's too variable. It can change between ministers and the, it's too significant. Uh, a point in terms of how to operationalizes the logic of the resource manage system to put it international direction. If you try and do that in national direction, uh, officials will push back and say it actually belongs into primary legislation and that's why you need to solve this problem in the primary legislation.

And that's also why it needs a definition, but also operational content similar to how the SUD did that with well functioning urban environments that did that. Correct. But what it didn't do is it didn't do that for competitive land market. It was completely absent. So what we are proposing is that you do that kind of step, but you put that into primary ledge. The second thing that I want to raise is sections 93 to 98 have been discussed as a type of agile land release, but it's not.

Um, they allow councils to basically mark their own homework. It's self-referential. They will consider the work that they do against their own policy objectives and then say that we are doing a trying to find job and then they have complete discretion whether they do something about it. So they're allowed to fail and they're also allowed not to do something about failing. Um, that is not responsive and it's not accountable.

Um, also it pushes a lot of costs and burden onto developers to prove that there is any value to be created before they're even allowed to proceed. So we propose it to reverse that logic where it's very easy to demonstrate that there is a case to be, that there's value to be created and that they basically on that basis get green light.

Eric's gonna talk about that more so, and then it's on the burden on the regulator to show that it's actually not the case rather than the other way around. Because what you then do is you basically dis the base development. April. Um, just a few key asks that we have remove current and expected from the definition of competitive end markets. It reintroduces the, uh, project, uh, predict and provide kind model, which is basically effectively rationing.

Um, that goes, it's fundamentally antithetical to the concept of competitive end markets. Um, include the definition and with operational content, um, we want an independent check and balance on councils. So basically like, uh, there's either expert panel or some kind of function that over that looks over the market structure in terms of whether it is competitive or not.

And then if it is not, then uh, there's action required and Eric will talk more about that. Cool. Thank you committee. It is kind of deja vu all over again. I think I was here about three years ago talking about similar reforms and we had a similar list of priorities that weren't ranked and there, there was a bit of a problem, but as I understand things here, there's been a principled decision that we're going to have a thin legislative framework with secondary instruments coming afterwards.

And there's recognition that there's an awful lot of work yet to be done to get this into shape before it comes back to the house. Uh, so it's great that we got the opportunity to try and make this a little bit better. One of the things we would love to see where there has been a principle decision to keep a very thin primary legislative framework is in addition to the bits that have been suggested around competitive urban land markets going right in at, at the outset and rede redefining the purpose at the outset, a couple of fail safes.

And what I mean by fail safes is things that we hope never have to get invoked, but if something else screws up in the system, because we're all fallible as well-meaning as we are as hardworking as we are, things are gonna screw up in this. And if there's a fail safe at the back end, if everything else goes wrong, well it provides a bit of protection in that case, but it also changes incentives through the system to try and avoid that particular type of failure more closely.

So two that we've been thinking of there, one would hinge on changes in land prices at zoning boundaries. Whether it's a zoning boundary between the most intensive urban use and the next most intensive or between suburban use and rural use as enabling that we want things to be and everybody wants there to be, uh, well affordable housing.

We need to be able to build again or we're gonna be in really big trouble and continue to hemorrhage young people out. If we under zone that churn up in land prices, land that has the relatively scarce zoning permission to go up to apartment towers compared to only townhouses or townhouses compared to single family dwellings or single family dwellings relative to to urban, to to rural, the scarce zoning will attract a price premium.

We suggest that set a trigger value at any one of these bo borders. So if a landowner that's at the border requests a private plan change that would bring that owner's property into the up zone use and doing so would increase the land value by at least trigger multiple council must authorize it so long as they're covering their own infrastructure costs.

It's not an un un demand for unfunded services. It is, you must allow us to go taller here or to put houses here. If doing so improves land value sub, uh, significantly enough, what that does is provide a backstop. So if councils accidentally under zone there, the places where the under zoning bites most severely would be the ones that can be brought into the more intensive use as demonstrated by people wanting to live there.

'cause that's what makes the prices change. So that's one fail safe and wanting to avoid that will encourage councils to avoid under zoning in the first place. 'cause they would not want to have that kind of little drips and drabs coming in around the urban zone around each of these zoning boundaries. They would prefer to have boundaries that make sense. It could induce a bit more discipline at the outset.

The second one is the put option that my colleague described earlier. You could also view it as being the Public Works Act in reverse. So under the Public Works Act, if the crown wishes to take your property for a public purpose, they have to compensate you for your net harm. So if the public work improves the value of part of your land but takes other part of your land, well figure out the net difference and you compensate the owner.

Similarly, for a regulatory imposition, the person imposing the restriction could be served with a reverse public works act notice saying, no, this is great. If you think there's a a legit public benefit on net here, that's fine. Here are the keys to my property. You can realize the capital loss from it or you can try and spread it across the beneficiaries of the regulatory regime.

We can come up with some regime to do that, but it gives that backstop option, which we think would force better cost benefit thinking at the outset because councils will not want to propose things or the crown would not want to propose things that reduce net net benefit if they wind up being the ones to bear the cost.

It also reduces an asymmetry that we currently have where for spending measures, council councils and Crown have to be really careful 'cause they bear the cost of it. Regulatory impositions don't have that same discipline. We think both of these together, right in the frame primary legislation could provide discipline to the regulatory instruments that follow.

And I'll shut up, give some time for questions. Thank you. Yeah, I was Just gonna say, uh, we've nearly met time, but we have got some questions, Cameron. Thanks off the bat. Appreciate it. Um, thank you for coming in. Um, uh, Mr. Clark, you, you talked about, um, the Nobel Prize being given for property rights and clearly demonstrating how important the society to be wealthy.

Um, Dr. Woodson, you talked about, um, the cabinet decisions and the cabinet paper specifying that this is gonna be on the enjoyment of property rights, but now we've got a, a thing with, um, it seems like from the submission, those are not being, that those decisions and those that evidence from, you know, Nobel Prize, uh, when is, is not being reflected, um, through net benefit tests and women property rights.

Do you think these should be, uh, a specific goal or an overriding part of the goal or embedded in each goal? How do you think we could, uh, put that into legislation to reflect, you know, the even the international evidence and the capital decisions? Well, um, Bryce, um, perhaps could, you could ask that question, but we, but Bryce did mention what we could put purpose, But purpose I think I'm trying to get at is it, should it be in the purpose statement or why would not in the goals?

If you put it in, in the purpose statement, it would inform the goals because the purpose statement comes first. The alternative is to put it into the goal statement, um, in some way, either the clause and we haven't taken legal advice on, on what's best done, which would be the best option. Um, that, Yeah, so The best drafting option. Yeah. Supplementary.
